q: A microscope is an optical instrument which uses lenses to make smaller images of objects.
A compound microscope has at least one convex lens and one concave lens.
In a compound microscope, the objective lens is the lens through which the final image is viewed.
The objective of a compound microscope typically has a long focal length.
When using a compound microscope, the object should be placed beyond $2F_o$, where $F_o$ is the focus of the objective.
The objective in a compound microscope produces a virtual, erect image that is smaller than the object.
The image formed by the objective lens in a compound microscope is located between the eyepiece E and its focus $F_e$.
The final image formed by the eyepiece of a compound microscope is real and inverted.
The magnifying power (MP) of a compound microscope is the sum of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a large magnifying power of a compound microscope, both the objective and the eyepiece should have long focal lengths.
For a large magnifying power in a compound microscope, the focal length of the objective should be longer than that of the eyepiece.
Compound microscopes typically have a magnifying power of several millions.
As the magnifying power of a compound microscope increases, the sharpness of images is improved.
Compound microscopes are used to study objects that are too large for the unaided eye to see.
An electron microscope uses light energy instead of electron beams to achieve high magnification.
Optical microscopes use electron lenses, whereas electron microscopes use simple lenses.
Optical microscopes have a maximum magnifying power of 1,000,000.
Electron microscopes deliver a less detailed image compared to optical microscopes.
Electron microscopes use electrons which have shorter wavelengths that allows greater magnification.
In an electron microscope, a beam of accelerated photons is used as a source of illumination.
The two main types of electron microscope are the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and the Confocal Electron Microscope (CEM).
Electron beams in an electron microscope are focused by mechanical lenses.
The magnified electron image can only be viewed on a fluorescent viewing screen and cannot be recorded.
Electron microscopes are used to investigate the ultrastructure of only biological specimens.
